Q1: All four are critical, but step-by-step procedural instruction and error recovery are the top priorities — citizens mostly contact us after something went wrong (ditolak, berkas kurang, NIK tidak terdaftar). Referral to the correct office is also essential because jurisdiction matters: Dukcapil vs Imigrasi vs DJP vs OSS-RBA are commonly confused. One we'd add: document prerequisite checking before the user even starts (what to bring, what format, e-materai requirements), since that prevents most rejections in the first place.
Q2: Yes, regional variation is unavoidable for us — kelurahan and kabupaten-level procedures genuinely differ, and DKI has its own Jakipedia/JakEVO flows that don't apply in, say, Sleman or Makassar. At minimum the bot must not confidently give Jakarta-specific steps to a user in East Nusa Tenggara. Honestly I'm not sure SEA-HELM captures this at all — I'd assume it tests general Indonesian, not procedural knowledge localized to pemda level.
Q3: Yes, this distinction is critical and frankly is the whole point for us. A plausible-sounding but outdated answer (e.g., old DJP e-Filing flow before Coretax, or pre-UU Adminduk KTP rules) is worse than an 'I don't know' — it exposes the ministry to liability. For recently changed guidance the system should defer: cite the regulation or SE number, timestamp the guidance, and route to the official channel rather than improvise. We'd also need this to comply with PP 71/2019 on data handling.
Q4: It needs to understand informal and regional-flavored input on the comprehension side — citizens will type 'bang gimana caranya urus KTP ilang' or Sundanese-inflected phrasing, and the bot must map that to the formal procedure. On the output side we want consistent, polite formal Bahasa Indonesia with clear plain-language explanation, not bureaucratese and not slang. Whether SEA-HELM evaluates this comprehension-vs-generation asymmetry, I honestly don't know.
Q5: It has to be a combination — Dukcapil/DJP civil servants for procedural correctness, our ministry's legal team for regulatory compliance, but ordinary citizens for whether the answer is actually usable. And yes, a technically correct answer written in impenetrable bureaucratic Indonesian is a failure in our context; accessibility for low-education and non-Jakarta users is an explicit KPI. I hadn't thought about benchmark annotator demographics before, but now that you raise it, if SEA-HELM annotators were all university-educated Jakartans that's a real gap for us.
